title: Frontend Engineering
cardSubtitle: Frontend engineering work on consumer surfaces, APIs, and performance/accessibility optimization for DirecTV and Warner Bros.
subtitle: Frontend engineering at DirecTV and, before that, Warner Bros.

sections.body: From late 2020 to mid-2025: frontend engineering, first at Warner Bros. and then at DirecTV. The DirecTV stretch was on the consumer site - the public-facing surfaces customers interact with when shopping for or managing service.

sections.title: Channel lineup
sections.body: The channel lineup is one of the more trafficked surfaces on the site, and one of the heavier ones. A server-side rendering refactor restructured how it loaded - content arriving sooner, behaving more predictably once there. The shift was measurable across the usual Core Web Vitals.

image.alt: The DirecTV channel lineup page after the SSR refactor.

sections.title: Accessibility
sections.alt: Health-score averages across four DirecTV properties.
sections.caption: A multi-month effort across four properties to bring the site into WCAG 2.2 conformance. Manual audits, automated scans, remediation coordinated across teams - the slow, unglamorous kind of work.

sections.title: Tooling for the team
sections.body: Some of the more useful work was infrastructure rather than features. The shared component library moved to independent versioning so teams could pull updates without coordinating releases. A small in-browser tool let engineers and designers preview components in context without a staging deploy.

image.alt: An in-page component preview tool showing the channel lineup in an inspection view.

sections.title: Other surfaces
sections.intro: A partial inventory of other things I shipped or contributed to:
items: Marketing components - countdown timers, reward callouts

sections.title: Before that
sections.body: Warner Bros., 2020-2023. Internal applications for production teams - casting payments, script management, asset tracking. Mostly solo ownership end-to-end, occasionally directing offshore engineers. The work taught me how to talk about software with people who weren't engineers, which has been useful since.
